question: how do gut bacteria produce serotonin
title: How Do Gut Bacteria Produce Serotonin?
metaDescription: About 90% of your body's serotonin is made in your gut — not your brain. Scotty breaks down the science of how gut bacteria manufacture the neurotransmitter that controls your mood, digestion, and sleep.
content:
# How Do Gut Bacteria Produce Serotonin?

Most people think serotonin lives in the brain. They think it's that "happy chemical" their doctor mentioned when they talked about antidepressants. And yeah, serotonin matters a whole lot for mood. But here's what blew my mind when I started digging into this: about 90% of the serotonin in your body is made in your gut. Not your brain. Your gut.

And your gut bacteria are running the show.

This isn't fringe science. This isn't some wellness blog trying to sell you something. This is peer-reviewed research from major institutions — including a 2025 study published in Cell Reports that identified the specific bacteria responsible. The gut-brain connection runs a lot deeper than most of us were ever taught, and once you understand it, everything about food starts to look different.

## What Serotonin Actually Does (And Why It's Not Just About Mood)

Serotonin is a neurotransmitter — a chemical messenger that neurons use to talk to each other. Most people associate it with happiness and depression. But serotonin does a lot more than regulate how you're feeling on a Tuesday morning.

In your gut, serotonin controls motility. That's the rhythmic muscle contractions that move food through your digestive system. It coordinates the timing and strength of those contractions, which is a big deal. When serotonin signaling in the gut goes sideways, you get dysmotility — and that can mean constipation, diarrhea, bloating, and the whole suite of symptoms associated with irritable bowel syndrome.

Serotonin also plays a role in your immune function, your sleep cycle, your appetite, and your pain perception. It's not a single-purpose molecule. It's a system-wide coordinator. And for most of us, the factory producing it isn't upstairs in our skull — it's downstairs, in the microbiome.

The research connecting gut bacteria to serotonin synthesis is building fast. A 2024 review in Food Science and Nutrition looked at the serotonin-probiotics-gut health axis and found consistent evidence that the gut microbiota plays a central role in regulating host serotonin levels, particularly through interactions with the enterochromaffin cells that line your intestinal wall.

## The Bacteria Doing the Work

For a long time, scientists knew that gut bacteria influenced serotonin production — but they didn't know which ones, or exactly how. A 2025 study published in Cell Reports changed that.

Researchers identified two specific bacteria: Limosilactobacillus mucosae and Ligilactobacillus ruminis. Both are strains of Lactobacillus — the same family of bacteria found in fermented foods like kimchi, sauerkraut, and yogurt. These bacteria produce serotonin by decarboxylating 5-hydroxytryptophan, which is 5-HTP, a precursor molecule. The study also found that microbe-to-microbe interactions matter — these two bacteria seem to work together, suggesting serotonin synthesis in the gut is a collaborative process, not a solo act.

Other genera associated with gut serotonin production include Escherichia, Enterococcus, and Streptococcus — though the Lactobacillus connection is particularly exciting for those of us in the fermented foods space, because it means what you eat directly affects this process.

A 2023 Springer study in Naunyn-Schmiedeberg's Archives of Pharmacology confirmed that gut microbiome composition has a significant, personalized effect on serotonin metabolism. The implication? Two people eating the same diet can have meaningfully different serotonin levels based on the bacterial communities in their gut.

Your gut bugs aren't just passengers. They're a little bit like a factory floor crew — and the quality of what you feed them determines what they're able to build.

## The Gut-Brain Axis and Why This Matters Beyond Digestion

The communication pathway between your gut and your brain is called the gut-brain axis. It runs both directions. Your brain talks to your gut — that's why anxiety causes stomach upset and stress makes your digestion go haywire. But your gut also talks to your brain, primarily through the vagus nerve, and serotonin is one of the main signals moving in that direction.

Here's the thing about that 90% figure. Most of the serotonin produced in your gut doesn't cross the blood-brain barrier — it stays in the enteric nervous system, the nervous system of the gut itself. But it still influences brain function indirectly through vagal signaling, through inflammatory pathways, through the behavior of immune cells that traffic between the gut and the rest of the body.

A 2024 narrative review published in PMC looked at the correlation between gut microbiota and neurotransmitters and found that altered microbial composition — dysbiosis — was consistently linked to disrupted serotonin signaling, which in turn correlated with anxiety, depression, and cognitive issues.

Gabe Brown talks a lot about the interconnection of biological systems in soil. How you can't treat the soil as a collection of separate chemical inputs — it's a web, and everything affects everything else. The gut works the same way. Serotonin isn't just a chemical in a capsule. It's the output of a living biological system. Mess with the system, you mess with the output.

That's why Albert Howard's whole approach — feed the biology, not the chemistry — applies as much to human health as it does to farming.

## What Fermented Foods Have to Do With It

If Lactobacillus strains are among the key producers of gut serotonin, and fermented foods are a primary source of live Lactobacillus bacteria, then the connection is pretty direct.

When I ferment vegetables — kimchi, sauerkraut, fermented beets — what I'm doing is cultivating a living ecosystem of lactic acid bacteria. Those bacteria survive the journey through your stomach better than most probiotic supplements because they're housed in an acidic food matrix that protects them. When they reach your intestines, they join the community that's already there.

Clinical research backs the connection. A UCLA Health study looked at how serotonin and antidepressants interact with gut microbiota and confirmed the bidirectional nature of the relationship — your microbiome composition affects serotonin, and serotonin levels in turn affect which bacteria thrive in your gut. It's a feedback loop.

That's why I always say: you have a separate organism within you. Your gut bacteria are not you. They're a different community that you cultivate — or fail to cultivate — with every meal. Feed them living food, and they'll do the biological work that keeps you well. Feed them ultra-processed garbage, and that factory floor grinds to a halt.

You want to support serotonin production naturally? Start with your gut. Feed it fermented vegetables. Feed it fiber-rich raw foods. Feed it the kind of food that those bacteria have been eating for millions of years. The brain-gut conversation doesn't start in your skull — it starts in what you put on your plate.

## The Bottom Line

Gut bacteria produce serotonin primarily through a two-step process: certain strains — especially Lactobacillus species like L. mucosae and L. ruminis — convert the precursor 5-HTP into serotonin through decarboxylation. This process depends heavily on which bacteria are present and how they interact with each other and with the cells lining your intestinal wall.

The implications run deep. Gut dysbiosis — an imbalanced microbial community — can disrupt serotonin production and signaling, contributing to IBS, mood disorders, and systemic inflammation. Repopulating the gut with diverse, healthy bacteria through fermented foods and a fiber-rich diet is one of the most direct ways to support the biology behind this process.

The gut isn't just a digestive organ. It's a neurotransmitter factory. Treat it like one.

---

*Sources:*

- [Identification of human gut bacteria that produce bioactive serotonin and promote colonic innervation — Cell Reports (2025)](https://www.cell.com/cell-reports/fulltext/S2211-1247(25)01205-7)
- [Exploring the serotonin-probiotics-gut health axis — Food Science & Nutrition (2024)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/fsn3.3826)
- [Effect of gut microbiome on serotonin metabolism: a personalized treatment approach — Naunyn-Schmiedeberg's Archives of Pharmacology (2023)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00210-023-02762-5)
- [The correlation between gut microbiota and neurotransmitters and mental disorders — PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10843545/)
- [Study shows how serotonin and antidepressants affect the gut's microbiota — UCLA Health](https://www.uclahealth.org/news/release/study-shows-how-serotonin-and-a-popular-antidepressant-affect-the-guts-microbiota)
